title: Salo
subtitle: Ukrainian salt-cured pork fat with garlic
excerpt: Thick-cut pork belly cured in coarse salt with garlic, bay, and black pepper for three days. Salo is Ukrainian and Russian peasant food at its most elemental: sliced thin, eaten cold on dark bread with raw onion and a shot of horilka.
sourceNotes: Based on the salo curing tradition as documented in Olia Hercules, 'Mamushka: Recipes from Ukraine and Beyond' (2015) and cross-referenced with traditional Ukrainian and Russian household curing practices. Salo is one of the oldest preserved foods of the Slavic tradition.

content.text: Salo is fat, nothing more than pork belly or back fat rubbed with salt, garlic, and black pepper and left in the cold for three days. It emerges from the cure firm, almost white, with a clean rendered-fat flavour and a mild pickle-like sharpness from the salt. Sliced thin against the grain and laid on black bread with a ring of raw onion, it is one of those foods where the pleasure is entirely in the texture and the contrast: cold fat, chewy bread, sharp onion.

The best cut is pork belly with a thick fat cap and minimal lean meat, the fat is the point. Back fat works as well but is harder to find in UK supermarkets; belly from a good butcher is the easier route. The skin stays on; it firms up during curing and is eaten with the rest.
This is dry-curing at its simplest. No brine, no weight, no special equipment. Salt and time are the only tools.

items.name: Pork belly
items.prepNote: skin on, fat cap at least 3 cm thick

items.name: Sea salt flakes
items.prepNote: coarse sea salt or kosher salt

content.text: Mix {{sea-salt-flakes}} of coarse salt with {{black-pepper}} of cracked black pepper and {{bay-leaves}} of crumbled bay leaves. Add {{garlic}} of sliced garlic and combine.

Pat {{pork-belly}} of pork belly dry. Rub the cure mixture all over the fat, sides, and skin, the fat side gets the thickest coating. Wrap tightly in cling film or lay in a non-reactive container with a lid. Refrigerate for 3 days. Flip the piece once at day 2.

After 3 days, wipe off the excess salt and garlic. The salo is ready when the fat is firm throughout and the surface is slightly tacky. Slice as thin as you can manage, a sharp knife kept cold helps. Serve immediately on dark rye bread or black bread with raw onion rings.

items.fix: 3 days is usually correct. If curing longer (5 to 7 days), soak slices in cold water for 20 minutes before serving to draw out excess salt.
items.cause: Over-cured, or salt rubbed in too aggressively on the lean meat side

items.fix: Ensure the fridge is below 4°C. The fat cap should be at least 3 cm thick; thinner cuts cure but remain softer.
items.cause: Fridge temperature too high, or pork belly too thin
items.symptom: Fat is soft rather than firm

items.fix: Start with very fresh pork and ensure all surfaces are clean. A faint sour-salt smell is normal; a putrid or ammonia smell means discard.
items.cause: Meat not fresh enough at the start, or contamination during curing

items.fix: Wipe the garlic off after 36 hours and re-wrap. The flavour infuses quickly.
items.cause: Garlic left on during the full cure

content.text: Salo is, in Ukraine, something close to a national symbol, the subject of festivals, proverbs, and a certain amount of affectionate mythology. It is also straightforwardly peasant food: cured pork fat was the preserved protein of the cold months, made when pigs were slaughtered in autumn and kept through winter. The same tradition exists across Russia, Poland, and the Baltic states, each with its own name and slight variation in seasoning. In Ukraine it appears at every celebration table, sliced thin and arranged on dark bread alongside horilka (Ukrainian grain spirit). It is not a dish that requires skill; it requires patience.
